content.title: Uniform Continuity
content.content:
A function $f: A \to \mathbb{R}$ is **uniformly continuous** 
on $A$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that
\[
|x - y| < \delta \text{ and } x, y \in A \implies |f(x) - f(y)| < \varepsilon.
\]

content.text:
The key difference from ordinary continuity: $\delta$ depends only on 
$\varepsilon$, not on the particular point.

content.title: Uniformly Continuous
content.solution:
$|f(x) - f(y)| = |3x - 3y| = 3|x - y|$. Given $\varepsilon > 0$, choose 
$\delta = \varepsilon/3$; this works for all $x, y$.

content.title: Not Uniformly Continuous
content.problem: $f(x) = 1/x$ is continuous but *not* uniformly continuous on $(0, 1)$.
content.solution:
Consider $x_n = 1/n$ and $y_n = 1/(n+1)$. Then $|x_n - y_n| = 1/(n(n+1)) \to 0$, 
but
\[
|f(x_n) - f(y_n)| = |n - (n+1)| = 1.
\]
No single $\delta$ works for $\varepsilon = 1$.

content.title: Not Uniformly Continuous
content.problem: $f(x) = x^2$ is not uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R}$.
content.solution:
Take $x_n = n$ and $y_n = n + 1/n$. Then $|x_n - y_n| = 1/n \to 0$, but
\[
|f(x_n) - f(y_n)| = |n^2 - (n + 1/n)^2| = |2 + 1/n^2| \to 2 \neq 0. \qedhere
\]

content.title: Continuous Functions on Compact Sets Are Uniformly Continuous
content.content:
If $K$ is compact and $f: K \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous, then $f$ is 
uniformly continuous on $K$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose $f$ is not uniformly continuous. Then for some $\varepsilon_0 > 0$, 
for every $\delta = 1/n$, there exist $x_n, y_n \in K$ with $|x_n - y_n| < 1/n$ 
but $|f(x_n) - f(y_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0$.

Since $K$ is compact, $(x_n)$ has a convergent subsequence $x_{n_k} \to c \in K$. 
Since $|x_{n_k} - y_{n_k}| < 1/n_k \to 0$, we also have $y_{n_k} \to c$.

By continuity, $f(x_{n_k}) \to f(c)$ and $f(y_{n_k}) \to f(c)$. Thus 
$|f(x_{n_k}) - f(y_{n_k})| \to 0$, contradicting 
$|f(x_{n_k}) - f(y_{n_k})| \geq \varepsilon_0$.

content.title: Lipschitz Implies Uniform Continuity
content.content:
If $f$ satisfies $|f(x) - f(y)| \leq M|x - y|$ for some constant $M$ and 
all $x, y$ in the domain, then $f$ is uniformly continuous.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Continuous Extension
content.content:
If $f: (a, b) \to \mathbb{R}$ is uniformly continuous, then $f$ extends to 
a continuous function $\tilde{f}: [a, b] \to \mathbb{R}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
We define $\tilde{f}(a) = \lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$. We must show this limit 
exists.

Let $(x_n)$ be any sequence in $(a, b)$ with $x_n \to a$. Since $(x_n)$ is 
Cauchy and $f$ is uniformly continuous, $(f(x_n))$ is Cauchy (for any 
$\varepsilon$, the corresponding $\delta$ makes $|f(x_n) - f(x_m)| < \varepsilon$ 
for large $n, m$). Thus $(f(x_n))$ converges.

By the sequential criterion, $\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$ exists. Similarly at $b$.
